Art and music threaded throughout the graduation weekend. We walked through the senior art studios and photography lab, admiring the innovative collages, black-and-white photography, and textured prints exploring a range of themes: identity, relationship, nature, and design. At a showcase for graduating film students, we watched short films exploring interpersonal relationships, the impact of a hoarder’s death on her daughter, and an Oberlin conductor’s connections between his Venezuelan roots and the music of Dmitri Shostakovich, who composed under the thumb of the Soviet government. 

One short documentary about couples who met late in life, filmed at the Kendal retirement community abutting the campus, struck me in its portrayal of how these relationships can be ones of convenience or deep love, depending on the situation. I felt surprised that a college student would find old-age love a compelling topic, and that the filmmaker succeeded in drawing out 80-somethings to discuss their sex lives, loneliness, and other intimate topics. Talking later to the student, I discovered that her professor had encouraged her to trim the film to 15 minutes, but she couldn’t bear to cut any more portions of her interviews.

In the film showcase and throughout the campus, visual arts shone, as well: delicate animations that conveyed the passing of time and eye-catching graphics and murals. Our oldest daughter remarked that students generally seemed engaged in conversation, not buried in their phones. When festivities hit a lull, I’d often turn around to find my son and his friends engaged in a game of cards or Scattergories. 

Their house seemed like a revolving door of housemates, relatives, neighbors, and friends, including recent graduates returning to celebrate the next crop of Obies. Everyone was unfailingly friendly and kind, greeting me and my parents with interest, engaging Jamie’s sisters in conversation, and open to getting to know anyone who happened to be in the same space. When tables or counters became crowded with dirty plates and cups, there always seemed to be a student taking out the trash or doing dishes. And they cheered each other on by attending each other’s screenings, recitals, concerts, sports games, and open studios.

I left the weekend feeling unexpectedly moved by the other students, in addition to seeing my own accept his diploma, of course. The thousands of 20-somethings on this campus aren’t alone in creating art and music that probes who we are as a society and what our relationships mean, or in taking action to change their communities. This is the generation that walked out of elementary school to protest gun violence and launched the careers of David Hogg and Greta Thunberg. This is what young people have always done, push human society to be the best version of itself. It’s often when times are the darkest and most uncertain that we turn to them for comfort and hope. Whether the writings of Anne Frank in the Holocaust or the voice of Joan Baez in the Vietnam War era, these voices carry unexpected and needed messages. The answer is in our relationships, in our commitment to and caring for each other. 

As author Rebecca Solnit argues, the hero is often the collective, not a lone charismatic figure. We ignore at our peril “that individual actors and collective forces can suddenly emerge or implode, that history often takes sharp turns, that something that has held for decades or centuries can suddenly snap,” she writes.

Ultimately, none of us know what the future holds. We are building it in the present, every day. With these idealistic, creative, community-minded young people joining the ranks of changemakers, I feel optimistic for the first time in a while.

title: Viral Infection Found to Trigger Parkinson’s Brain Damage
summary: A new study demonstrates that a non-toxic viral infection via TMEV triggers the exact dopaminergic neuron destruction and progressive gait degradation seen in human Parkinson's disease, establishing a vital new animal model to study the natural environmental origins of neurodegeneration.

title: Comforting Others Is a Cultural Trait, Not a Universal Instinct
summary: Comforting others is a culturally bounded motive rather than a universal human instinct. While individualistic societies actively try to eliminate a peer's distress to foster intimacy, collectivistic cultures accommodate negative emotions, viewing them as vital instruments for self-reflection and relationship cohesion.

title: Nanosensor Separates Autism From Intellectual Disability
summary: Scientists demonstrates that a carbon-fiber nanosensor can differentiate Autism Spectrum Disorder from Intellectual Disability by measuring real-time nitric oxide production in undifferentiated patient stem cells, even when both conditions share identical genetic mutations.

title: Study Maps the Global Matrix of Self-Control
summary: A massive, 77-nation collaborative study is surveying 15,000 participants globally to map out how different cultural and situational backgrounds alter intertemporal choices, the critical decisions balancing immediate gratification against long-term future rewards.

title: Brain Tissue Patterns and Genetics Drive MS Progression
summary: A comprehensive study of 287 MS brain tissue donors from the Netherlands Brain Bank demonstrates that multiple sclerosis progression variability is driven by specific pathological features, such as broad rim lesions and myelin repair failures, which are directly modulated by an individual's genetic background.

title: A Universal Model of Childhood Mind Development
summary: Participants consistently categorize mental growth into an early-emerging "Perceptual–Experiential" nature dimension and a later-developing "Reflective–Evaluative" nurture dimension, showing that a synchronized cognitive model underpins global perceptions of childhood and human potential.

title: Arthritis Drug Found to Reduce Excessive Alcohol Use
summary: An FDA-approved rheumatoid arthritis drug targeting the IL-6 receptor significantly reduces excessive alcohol consumption in alcohol-dependent female mice. The treatment functions by blocking neuroinflammatory pathways that suppress the GABAergic braking system in the central amygdala, a mechanism corroborated by elevated IL-6 expression found in postmortem human AUD brain tissue.

title: Adolescent Social Isolation Permanently Blunts Adult Empathy
summary: A brief two-week period of social isolation during adolescence permanently destroys an animal's adult capacity to sense emotional distress in peers and perform consolation grooming. This socio-emotional blindness persists despite later re-socialization, whereas adult isolation leaves emotional discrimination fully intact.

title: Arc Protein Found to Spread Toxic Tau in Alzheimer’s
summary: The native brain protein Arc mediates the intercellular transmission of toxic Tau via extracellular vesicles in Alzheimer's disease. While Arc helps sick neurons survive longer by expelling toxic Tau aggregates, it simultaneously promotes the spread of pathology to healthy cells, identifying the extracellular space as a prime "mid-flight" therapeutic target.

title: Hearing Aids Linked to 23% Lower Dementia Risk in Epilepsy
summary: Hearing aid use is associated with a 23% lower risk of dementia exclusively in adults with both epilepsy and hearing loss, likely due to the critical preservation of depleted cognitive reserve.

title: Reading Is the Ultimate Cognitive Enhancer
summary: A new study demonstrates that reading acts as a powerful cognitive enhancer that fine-tunes visual systems to improve face recognition, expands working memory, and sharpens reasoning. The work notes that print reading evokes greater cognitive effort than screen reading due to psychological self-regulation, and cautions against the over-simplification of educational texts.

title: Dual Brain-Machine Interfaces Unlock Bionic Hand Kinesthesia
summary: A new study combining data from two distinct brain-machine interfaces has revealed that the human brain processes artificial movement sensations as coordinated, subconscious hand synergies rather than isolated muscle signals, paving the way for intuitive, bi-directional permanent prosthetic implants.

title: Learning English with English Clubs
summary: English Clubs are great tools for learning English.  Here are tips on how to start and organize your club.

title: Images Can Help You Retain Vocabulary
summary: On this Education Tips, we will discuss three methods for retaining vocabulary with the help of imagery. The methods include the use of pictures, mnemonics keywords and word maps.

title: Raise Your English Level with More Vocabulary
summary: Some studies have shown that for learners to fully understand a written work, they need to know 95 percent or more of the words in it. That means that if there are only five or more words you do not know in a 100-word text, you might not really understand it!

title: How to Write a Good Email
summary: Writing a good email can mean the difference between someone reading it and getting a response and someone ignoring it.

title: Scientists with Disabilities Seek Changes to Improve their Work
summary: Researchers with disabilities often face barriers that others do not. A recent outing in Southern California gave a group of disabled researchers a chance to discuss how they face their problems and aim for good results.

title: Scientists with Disabilities Seek Changes to Improve their Work
Make a Memory Palace to Improve Your English
summary: Sherlock Holmes is an imaginary private detective created by British writer Arthur Conan Doyle in the late 1880s. One of Sherlock Holmes’ great skills is a powerful memory. In a recent BBC television series, the character uses a memory technique called a “mind palace” or “memory palace.”

title: Students with Disabilities Praise Assistive Technology Using AI
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is being used to help students with visual, speech, language and hearing disabilities. Schools in the United States are developing methods for how to use AI and are trying out new applications.

title: Learn New Skills with Microcredentials
summary: When four years is too long to wait for learning a job skill, taking short courses that award microcredentials may help with getting a job.

title: Learn New Skills with Microcredentials
Students with Disabilities Praise Assistive Technology Using AI
Start New Year by Setting Reasonable Learning Goals
summary: A good way to start the new year is setting reasonable goals for learning English. Learn how goals help guide language learners and give them reasons to continue studying a language.

title: In Uganda, Cost of Attending School Keeps Children Home
summary: The World Bank says African countries south of the Sahara Desert have the highest rates of children out of school. In Uganda, parents say school costs are high, and they increase for many reasons. Schools regularly ask for additional money to pay for different projects.

title: In Uganda, Cost of Attending School Keeps Children Home
Learning How to Ask Questions
summary: Suzanne Meyer says that students need to practice question-asking activities. Question-asking activities help students to become better language learners. By practicing question-asking activities, students can improve their language skills and develop critical thinking skills.

title: Tips for Better Writing
summary: Everyone struggles with writing, however there are ways to ease your struggle when working on an essay or paper for class. Jennifer Ahern-Dodson, assistant professor of the Practice in Writing Studies at Duke University is here with some tips.

title: Improve Your Pronunciation By 'Shadowing' Others
summary: Many English learners who want to improve the way they pronounce words often think about how to make specific sounds. But English learners can better improve their pronunciation by shadowing – matching the rise and fall of a native speaker’s voice.

title: Learn about US Presidential Campaigns
summary: Every four years, the United States holds an election for the President in November. In the summer before the election, the political parties meet to nominate their candidates and tell the voters about their policies. Use this to learn or teach the expressions you might hear during an election year.

title: Learn about US Presidential Campaigns
Use a Language Corpus to Improve Your Vocabulary
summary: A language corpus is a powerful tool that can help learners improve their understanding and use of vocabulary. This article focuses on COCA, the Corpus of Contemporary American English.

title: Grammar Games at Home: Jeopardy
summary: With so many people stuck at home, Everyday Grammar will be offering ideas for games that you can play with your family or housemates to practice your English. Today's game is Jeopardy.

title: Common Adverbs in Conversation: Amplifiers, Downtoners
summary: In this Everyday Grammar program, we examine some of the most important adverbs that you will hear in everyday speech. There is even a special name for them. They are called amplifiers and downtoners.

title: Words Come and Go in English
Adverb and Adjective Choices in Conversation
summary: In today's Everyday Grammar, we will study how conversational grammar differs from written and formal grammatical structures. Americans often use adjective forms in place of adverbs. Americans also may use an adverb but choose not to say an –ly ending.

title: Reviewing Your Birthday Message
summary: In this week's Everyday Grammar, we revisit a birthday message from a listener.

title: Reviewing Your Birthday Message
Describing Your Birthday
summary: In this week’s Everyday Grammar, we talk about how to describe our birthdays.

title: Describing Your Birthday
Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part Four
summary: English has many patterns. Learning and mastering these patterns can help you improve your writing and speaking skills. Today's common pattern is the transitive verb pattern. There are several types of transitive verbs, but they all have one thing in common: a direct object.

title: Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part Three
summary: What are intransitive verbs and how are they used? Find out in the third part of our series about common sentence patterns in the English language.

title: Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part Three
Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part Two
summary: In this week's report, we are going to explore one pattern that is commonly used in academic writing. Academic writers often use it to judge an idea. The structure we are exploring starts with a noun followed by a "that clause."

title: Academic Writing: Common Sentence Patterns, Part One
summary: Today on Everyday Grammar, we explore two grammatical structures that you will see often in academic writing. These structures are used to evaluate ideas and evaluate actions.  Although common in academic writing, you might not want to use these structures in other types of writing.

title: Studying Sentence Patterns to Improve Your Writing, Part Two
summary: This week, we give you more information about a common sentence pattern in English: the linking verb pattern. Learning how to use linking verb patterns will help you improve your writing. You can think about creative ways to use them, or you can learn to recognize when you use them too often.

title: Studying Sentence Patterns to Improve Your Writing, Part One
summary: The English language has many patterns. By learning and understanding common sentence patterns, English learners can improve their writing skills.

title: Questions, Answers about Holiday Activities
summary: In today's report, we explore questions and answers about holiday activities.

title: Studying Sentence Patterns to Improve Your Writing, Part Two
Studying Sentence Patterns to Improve Your Writing, Part One
Grammar for Making New Year’s Resolutions
summary: Though we may celebrate the New Year holiday on different dates, people from many cultures make New Year’s resolutions. On today’s Everyday Grammar, we will show you how to talk about resolutions in English.

title: Grammar and Christmas
summary: Christmas is full of pleasant surprises and surprising disappointments. Americans use certain words to show these emotions. Common examples include "even," "still," and "actually."
